category_summary:
The AI community was jolted by **OpenAI** closing a [record **$122 billion funding round**](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-36b819ca1be2) at an **$852B valuation**, with **TheRundownAI** contextualizing the staggering revenue trajectory — $2B/month, 900M weekly users, and 50M paid subscribers.

- **John Carmack** delivered an exceptional [deep-dive review](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-0a31def148d8) of the **LeWorldModel** JEPA paper, offering rare first-principles technical analysis on world models for robotics
- **Andrej Karpathy** dominated discourse twice: sounding alarms on a major [**npm axios supply chain attack**](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-1d860391b1a4) (1.2M views) and proposing an influential [**LLM-as-CPU, Agent-as-OS** conceptual framework](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-d8949a2cc777) that resonated widely
- **Google** announced **Veo 3.1 Lite**, their most cost-efficient video generation model, signaling aggressive expansion in generative video
- **Anthropic's Claude Code** [source code leak](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-847d350d1d51) drew sharp criticism from **SVPino** about unchecked AI-generated code shipping to production
- **Andrew Ng** [published a lengthy policy argument](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-cb97413ab2f7) against anti-AI coalitions, backing federal preemption of state AI laws aligned with the White House framework

On the research and ecosystem front, **Research_FRI** [shared a comprehensive study](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-e2cac27c6876) showing economists expect major AI progress but muted near-term GDP impact. **Coinbase** provided a [striking real-world case study](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-695276596852) — cutting ticket-to-PR time from 8 days to 12 hours. **HuggingFace** CEO **Clement Delangue** marked a milestone with the [**TRL v1.0** release](/?date=2026-04-01&category=social#item-85360d6217e9) powering post-training for most open models.

items.title:
Paper review: LeWorldModel: Stable End-to-End Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture from Pixels
ht...
items.content:
Paper review: LeWorldModel: Stable End-to-End Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture from Pixels
https://t.co/TpFFnwPWkc

Nice clean github: https://t.co/HOuqEf0HaF

This is the application of the LeJEPA results to world models, trained offline on experience from three different robotics style tests with one to two million steps in each dataset. Re-states the benefits of the SigReg loss relative to prior world model approaches.

Uses ImageNet standard 224x224 RGB pixel input images with an unmodified ViT-Tiny vision transformer from HuggingFace to generate latents. One extra post-projection step is needed to give SigReg the necessary freedom to perturb the latents into independent gaussians, since ViT ends with a layernorm’d layer. Also tested with ResNet-18, which still performed well, but slightly worse.

Uses a 192 dimensional latent. Performance slightly dropped when doubling the latent size to 384; it would be nice to know if it was stable there, or if it continued worsening with excessive latents. There is a relationship between batch size and SIGReg, the larger latent may have improved performance if the batch size was increased.

The predictor is implemented as a ViT-S backbone – Why a vision transformer when the latent is flat? Uses a history of 3 sets of latents for two of the benchmarks and 1 for the other. Performance was markedly better with the “small” ViT model than the “tiny”, but the larger “base” model degraded notably, which is interesting.

Dropout of 0.1 on the predictor significantly improved performance. 0.2 was still better than 0.0, but 0.5 was worse.

Trained with a batch of 128 x 4 trajectories. I wish their training loss graphs were more zoomed in with grid lines.

Performs planning at test time instead of building a policy by training in imagination like Dreamer / Diamond. Rolls out 300 initially random sets of actions up to a planning horizon H of 5 (at frame-skip 5). Iterates up to 30 times using the Cross Entropy Method (CEM). The main paper body mentions using Model Predictive Control (MPC) strategy, where only the first K planned actions are executed before replanning, but appendix D says they execute all 5 planned actions.

After training, they probe the latent space to demonstrate that it does capture and represent physically meaningful quantities. They also implement a decoder from the latent space back to pixels – not used by the algorithms, but helpful to see what things the latent space is actually representing. They tested incorporating the reconstruction loss into training, but it hurt performance somewhat.

They wound up with a 0.1 lambda for SigReg, as opposed to 0.05 in the LeJEPA paper. 1024 sigreg projections, but observe the number has negligible impact

I like the JEPA framework, but so far my attempts to use it on Atari games with value functions have not matched my other efforts.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack

items.reasoning: Exceptional technical depth from a legendary engineer. Carmack's detailed paper review covers architecture specifics (ViT-Tiny, SigReg, latent dimensionality tradeoffs), planning methodology (CEM, MPC), and includes his own critical commentary and practical experience. 149K views, 660 likes. This is original, high-quality technical analysis of JEPA world models.

items.title: The anti-AI coalition continues to maneuver to find arguments to slow down AI progress. If someone h...
items.content:
The anti-AI coalition continues to maneuver to find arguments to slow down AI progress. If someone has a sincere concern about a specific effect of AI, for instance that it may lead to human extinction, I respect their intellectual honesty, even if I deeply disagree with their position. However, I am concerned about organizations that are surveying the public to find whatever messages will turn people against AI, and how the public reacts as these messages are spread by lobbyists or by politicians seeking to alarm constituents, companies pursuing regulatory capture or seeking to promote the power of their technology, and individuals seeking to gain attention or to profit by being provocative.

A large study (link in original article below; h/t to the AI Panic blog) by a UK group tested different messages that are designed to raise alarm about AI. Their study found that saying AI will cause human extinction has largely failed. Doomsayers were pushing this argument a couple of years ago, and fortunately our community beat it back. But AI-enabled warfare and environmental concerns resonate better. We should be prepared for a flood of messages (which is already underway) arguing against AI on these grounds. Further, job loss and harm to children are messages that motivate people to act.

To be clear, I find AI-enabled warfare alarming; we need to continue serious efforts to monitor and mitigate the environmental impact of AI; any job losses are tragic and hurt individuals and families; and as a father, I hold dearly the importance of every child’s welfare. Each of these topics deserves serious attention and treatment with the greatest of care.

But when anti-AI propagandists take a one-sided view of complex issues to benefit their own organizations at the expense of the public at large — for instance, when big AI companies argue that AI is dangerous to block the free distribution of open source projects that compete with their offerings — then we all lose.

For example, public perception of data centers’ environmental impact is already far worse than the reality — data centers are incredibly efficient for the work they do, and hampering their buildout will hurt rather than help the environment. While job loss is a real problem, the “AI washing” of layoffs — in which businesses that had over-hired during the pandemic blame AI for recent layoffs, although AI hasn’t yet affected their operations — has led to overblown fears about the impact of AI on employment.

Unfortunately, this sort of propaganda easily leads to regulations that create worse outcomes for everyone. For example, oil companies worked for years to create fear of nuclear energy. The result is that overblown concerns about the safety of nuclear power plants has stifled nuclear power development, leading to millions of premature deaths from air pollution that was caused by other energy sources and a massive increase in CO2 emissions. Let’s make sure overblown concerns about AI do not lead to a similar fate for the many people that would benefit from faster AI development.

Last week, the White House proposed a national legislative framework for AI. A key component is a federal preemption framework to prevent a patchwork of state regulations that hamper AI development. I support this. 

After failing to gain traction at the federal level, a lot of anti-AI propaganda has shifted to the state level. If just one of the 50 states passes a law that limits AI in an unproductive way, it could lead to stifling AI development across all the states and potentially across the globe. The White House proposal rightfully respects each state’s rights to control its own zoning, how it enforces general laws to protect consumers, and how it uses AI. But if a state were to pass laws that limit AI development, federal rules would preempt the state law.

The White House proposal remains a proposal for now. However, if the U.S. Congress enacts it, it will clear the way for ongoing efforts to develop AI in beneficial ways.

Where do we go from here? Let’s support limiting applications — those that use AI, and those that don’t — that harm people. When the anti-AI coalition argues against AI, in addition to considering the merits of the argument, I consider whether their position is consistent and persuasive, or if they are just promoting whatever concerns they think will sway the public at a given moment. And, let’s also keep using a scientific approach to weighing AI’s benefits against likely harms, so we don’t end up with overblown concerns that limit the benefits that AI can bring everyone.

[Original text with links: https://t.co/kfZY7mo0Mi ]

items.title: We completed the most comprehensive study of how economists and AI experts think AI will affect the ...
items.content:
We completed the most comprehensive study of how economists and AI experts think AI will affect the U.S. economy.

They predict major AI progress—but no dramatic break from economic trends: GDP growth rates similar to today's and a moderate decline in labor force participation.

However, when asked to consider what would happen in a world with extremely rapid progress in AI capabilities by 2030, they predict significant economic impacts by 2050:

• Annualized GDP growth of 3.5% (compared to 2.4% in 2025)
• A labor force participation rate of 55% (roughly 10 million fewer jobs)
• 80% of wealth held by the top 10% (highest since 1939)

🧵 Here's what we found:

Under rapid AI progress, GDP growth hits 3.5%, labor force participation drops to 55% (~10M fewer jobs), and top 10% would hold 80% of wealth by 2050.

items.title: New supply chain attack this time for npm axios, the most popular HTTP client library with 300M week...
items.content:
New supply chain attack this time for npm axios, the most popular HTTP client library with 300M weekly downloads.

Scanning my system I found a use imported from googleworkspace/cli from a few days ago when I was experimenting with gmail/gcal cli. The installed version (luckily) resolved to an unaffected 1.13.5, but the project dependency is not pinned, meaning that if I did this earlier today the code would have resolved to latest and I'd be pwned.

It's possible to personally defend against these to some extent with local settings e.g. release-age constraints, or containers or etc, but I think ultimately the defaults of package management projects (pip, npm etc) have to change so that a single infection (usually luckily fairly temporary in nature due to security scanning) does not spread through users at random and at scale via unpinned dependencies.

More comprehensive article:
https://t.co/EJAZbqAPIQ
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Karpathy reports a new npm supply chain attack on axios (300M weekly downloads), shares personal near-miss, and argues package managers need better defaults to prevent infection spread through unpinned dependencies.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (10K likes, 1.2M views). Karpathy providing firsthand account of a major supply chain vulnerability. Directly relevant to AI coding agents that run installs. Actionable security insight.

items.title: This was an amazing talk by @chintanturakhia who leads up AI-Native Engineering @coinbase. Got hyped...
items.content:
This was an amazing talk by @chintanturakhia who leads up AI-Native Engineering @coinbase. Got hyped after watching it. 

He talks about how Coinbase went all in. Their northstar was intent to prod in less than 4 hours. 

What they did: 
- Time from ticket to PR: 8 days to 12 mins 
- Time to merge: 168 hours to 16 hours
- Time to production: 7.8days to 1.8 days

They started with 5yr+ legacy codebase, long CI process, etc and Chintan walks through things that they changed along the way to help them move fast. 

Great takeaways
- Unify the context and give it to your agent. This is obvious but the context graph continues to be really important. 
- Unleash a superbuilder - aka someone to help build the factory.

Full talk linked below
items.author: @aparnadhinak
items.summary: Aparna Dhinakaran shares a talk by Coinbase's AI-Native Engineering lead about how they went all-in on AI: reduced ticket-to-PR time from 8 days to 12 minutes, time to production from 7.8 days to 1.8 days, using context graphs and 'superbuilders'.
items.reasoning: Concrete, quantified real-world case study of AI-driven engineering transformation at a major tech company. Good engagement (118 likes, 9.8K views). Actionable insights about AI in production engineering.
items.summary_html: Aparna Dhinakaran shares a talk by Coinbase's AI-Native Engineering lead about how they went all-in on AI: reduced ticket-to-PR time from 8 days to 12 minutes, time to production from 7.8 days to 1.8 days, using context graphs and 'superbuilders'.
items.content_html:
This was an amazing talk by @chintanturakhia who leads up AI-Native Engineering @coinbase. Got hyped after watching it.

He talks about how Coinbase went all in. Their northstar was intent to prod in less than 4 hours.

What they did:

  • Time from ticket to PR: 8 days to 12 mins
  • Time to merge: 168 hours to 16 hours
  • Time to production: 7.8days to 1.8 days

They started with 5yr+ legacy codebase, long CI process, etc and Chintan walks through things that they changed along the way to help them move fast.

Great takeaways

  • Unify the context and give it to your agent. This is obvious but the context graph continues to be really important.
  • Unleash a superbuilder - aka someone to help build the factory.

Full talk linked below

items.title:
OpenAI's revenue trajectory is one of the more absurd things in business history.

$1B annual -> wit...
items.content:
OpenAI's revenue trajectory is one of the more absurd things in business history.

$1B annual -> within a year of ChatGPT's launch.

$1B per quarter -> by end of 2024.

$2B per month -> now.

Growing 4x faster than Alphabet and Meta did at comparable stages.

900M weekly active users. 50M paid subscribers. Enterprise now 40%+ of revenue.

The ads pilot hit $100M ARR in under six weeks.

Now, they just closed the largest private funding round in Silicon Valley history at $122B.

The next chapter: everything consolidates. ChatGPT, Codex, browsing, agents.

Their framing: "a unified AI superapp." One agent-first experience. 

IPO expected by year-end.

items.title: What explains economists giving a high chance of substantial AI progress, yet having an overall beli...
items.content:
What explains economists giving a high chance of substantial AI progress, yet having an overall belief that economic outcomes won’t shift dramatically?

In their written rationales, economists cited the following reasons:
 • slow and uneven diffusion of AI across sectors
 • infrastructure bottlenecks (energy, chips, data centers)
 • demographic and geopolitical headwinds
 • long lags between the discovery of general-purpose technologies and measured productivity gains

The median view is closer to "AI will take a long time to show up in macroeconomic statistics and will offset demographic headwinds" than "AI won't matter at all."
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: Thread continuation: Explains why economists expect major AI progress but muted economic impact — slow diffusion, infrastructure bottlenecks (energy, chips, data centers), demographic headwinds, and historical lags between GPT discovery and productivity gains.
items.reasoning: Provides the reasoning behind the paradox of expected AI progress vs muted economic outcomes. The cited reasons (infra bottlenecks, slow diffusion) are substantive and reflect real-world constraints.
items.summary_html: Thread continuation: Explains why economists expect major AI progress but muted economic impact — slow diffusion, infrastructure bottlenecks (energy, chips, data centers), demographic headwinds, and historical lags between GPT discovery and productivity gains.

In their written rationales, economists cited the following reasons:

• demographic and geopolitical headwinds

The median view is closer to "AI will take a long time to show up in macroeconomic statistics and will offset demographic headwinds" than "AI won't matter at all."

items.title: @hammerhoundai @julianboolean_ No. Auto-regressive prediction produces a single path in the tree of ...
items.content:
@hammerhoundai @julianboolean_ No. Auto-regressive prediction produces a single path in the tree of possible sequences. 
At non-zero temperature (stochastic generation) the potential paths form a subtree of the full tree, or rather, a distribution over all paths in the tree. If you threshold all the paths above a certain probability, it's a subtree. 
The set of acceptable answers is also a subtree.
If a token takes you outside that subtree, YOU WILL NEVER RECOVER, precisely because it's a tree. 

Now, if you use non-auto-regressive tricks on top, like generating lots of symbol sequences and picking a "good" one (which many reasoning models do), then the argument does not apply the same way.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun provides most detailed explanation of his autoregressive divergence argument: LLM produces single path in tree, errors take you outside the correct subtree permanently. Notes that non-autoregressive tricks (like generating many sequences and picking best) circumvent this.
items.reasoning: Most comprehensive version of LeCun's argument in this thread. Important distinction between pure autoregressive and augmented systems. Explains why reasoning models work despite theoretical limits.
items.summary_html: LeCun provides most detailed explanation of his autoregressive divergence argument: LLM produces single path in tree, errors take you outside the correct subtree permanently. Notes that non-autoregressive tricks (like generating many sequences and picking best) circumvent this.
items.content_html:
@hammerhoundai @julianboolean_ No. Auto-regressive prediction produces a single path in the tree of possible sequences.

At non-zero temperature (stochastic generation) the potential paths form a subtree of the full tree, or rather, a distribution over all paths in the tree. If you threshold all the paths above a certain probability, it's a subtree.

The set of acceptable answers is also a subtree.

If a token takes you outside that subtree, YOU WILL NEVER RECOVER, precisely because it's a tree.

Now, if you use non-auto-regressive tricks on top, like generating lots of symbol sequences and picking a "good" one (which many reasoning models do), then the argument does not apply the same way.

items.title: The fact that you play a board game well doesn't make you an expert in AI (or any other field, like ...
items.content:
The fact that you play a board game well doesn't make you an expert in AI (or any other field, like politics).

The moment you test an LLM on your brand new ARC-AGI test, the LLM provider knows the tasks you used for the test, and they will finetune the model on these and similar tasks so that the second time they beat your benchmark better.

The fact that you added the "-AGI" prefix to the test doesn't change the fact that testing a neural network on an out-of-distribution example almost always results in a failure. It's machine learning 101.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov criticizes ARC-AGI benchmarks, arguing that LLM providers finetune on leaked test tasks and that testing neural networks OOD almost always fails - it's ML 101
items.reasoning: Substantive critique of ARC-AGI methodology from ML author with 15K views and 100+ likes. Raises important points about benchmark contamination and the misunderstanding of generalization.
items.summary_html: Burkov criticizes ARC-AGI benchmarks, arguing that LLM providers finetune on leaked test tasks and that testing neural networks OOD almost always fails - it's ML 101
items.content_html:
The fact that you play a board game well doesn't make you an expert in AI (or any other field, like politics).

The moment you test an LLM on your brand new ARC-AGI test, the LLM provider knows the tasks you used for the test, and they will finetune the model on these and similar tasks so that the second time they beat your benchmark better.

The fact that you added the "-AGI" prefix to the test doesn't change the fact that testing a neural network on an out-of-distribution example almost always results in a failure. It's machine learning 101.

items.title: Despite expecting significant AI progress, economists' overall (unconditional) forecasts for the eco...
items.content:
Despite expecting significant AI progress, economists' overall (unconditional) forecasts for the economy stay close to today's trends.

Median economist forecasts:
 • Annual GDP growth: 2.5% in 2030 and 2050 (compared to 2.4% in 2025)
 • Labor force participation rate: 61% in 2030, 58% in 2050 (compared to 62.6% in 2025)

But, in a ‘rapid’ AI progress world, economists expect larger shifts (see red lines in figures below).

items.title:
Some wild numbers from Quinnipiac's new AI poll:

51% of Americans surveyed use AI for research. Onl...
items.content:
Some wild numbers from Quinnipiac's new AI poll:

51% of Americans surveyed use AI for research. Only 21% trust it.

70% say AI will decrease jobs. But only 30% are concerned it will touch theirs.

Only 5% believe AI is being developed by people who represent their interests.

74% of Americans say the government isn't doing enough to regulate AI.

52% earning $200K+ say AI does more good than harm.

60% earning under $50K say the opposite. 

56% say they're confident they can spot AI fakes, while 28% have already shared one.

81% still want a human involved in their medical scans, even if AI is proven more accurate.

65% don't want a data center in their town.

And through all of this, overall AI usage went up 14%.

items.reasoning: Comprehensive public opinion data on AI with striking contradictions (usage up 14% despite low trust). Income divide in AI perception is an important signal. Data from credible pollster.

51% of Americans surveyed use AI for research. Only 21% trust it.

70% say AI will decrease jobs. But only 30% are concerned it will touch theirs.

Only 5% believe AI is being developed by people who represent their interests.

74% of Americans say the government isn't doing enough to regulate AI.

52% earning $200K+ say AI does more good than harm.

60% earning under $50K say the opposite.

56% say they're confident they can spot AI fakes, while 28% have already shared one.

81% still want a human involved in their medical scans, even if AI is proven more accurate.

65% don't want a data center in their town.

And through all of this, overall AI usage went up 14%.

items.title:
Economists expect substantial AI progress by 2030.

When asked to assign probabilities to slow, mode...
items.content:
Economists expect substantial AI progress by 2030.

When asked to assign probabilities to slow, moderate, and rapid AI progress scenarios, they predict a:

 • 39% chance of a slow progress scenario
 • 47% chance of a moderate progress scenario
 • 14% chance of a rapid progress scenario

Economists’ 61% chance of moderate or rapid AI progress by 2030 is roughly in line with other respondent groups (AI experts, superforecasters, and the general public).

Economists expect substantial AI progress by 2030.

When asked to assign probabilities to slow, moderate, and rapid AI progress scenarios, they predict a:

• 39% chance of a slow progress scenario

• 47% chance of a moderate progress scenario

• 14% chance of a rapid progress scenario

Economists’ 61% chance of moderate or rapid AI progress by 2030 is roughly in line with other respondent groups (AI experts, superforecasters, and the general public).

As you can see below, even moderate progress is a significant advance from present-day AI capabilities.

items.title:
@int_16h @julianboolean_ You didn't understand either. 
Yes, the independence of errors is an assump...
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun explains that errors in autoregressive settings are NOT recoverable because correct answers form a subtree - once you leave it, you can never return.
items.reasoning: Key technical insight about why autoregressive error correction is fundamentally limited. Tree structure argument is elegant and important.
items.summary_html: LeCun explains that errors in autoregressive settings are NOT recoverable because correct answers form a subtree - once you leave it, you can never return.
items.content_html:
@int_16h @julianboolean_ You didn't understand either.

Yes, the independence of errors is an assumption, which may or may not be reasonable.

No, errors are NOT RECOVERABLE in an auto-regressive setting because the set of correct answers form a subtree in the tree of all possible sequences. Once you get out of the "correct" subtree you CAN NEVER get back to it.

items.title: Most expert disagreement isn't about whether we get powerful AI systems—it's about what powerful AI ...
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI finds most economist disagreement about AI impact isn't about AI capabilities (5.2% of variance) but about economic outcomes given AI progress (16.1%) and individual uncertainty (78.7%).
items.reasoning: Excellent analytical finding: the key uncertainty isn't about what AI can do, but what it will do to the economy. This reframes the AI forecasting debate significantly. Methodologically rigorous.
items.summary_html: FRI finds most economist disagreement about AI impact isn't about AI capabilities (5.2% of variance) but about economic outcomes given AI progress (16.1%) and individual uncertainty (78.7%).
items.content_html:
Most expert disagreement isn't about whether we get powerful AI systems—it's about what powerful AI systems would do to the economy.

For economists' 2030 GDP growth forecasts:

• 78.7% of the total variation in forecasts is driven by the uncertainty that each economist has about what will happen in a given scenario;

• 16.1% of the total variance is driven by disagreement about economic outcomes conditional on a given level of AI progress;

• Only 5.2% of the variance is between scenarios—attributable to disagreement about AI capabilities themselves, as described by our (imperfect) scenarios.

We see similar patterns for all other outcomes, and across expert groups.

items.title: In a rapid AI progress scenario—which economists assigned a 14% probability to—economists expect muc...
items.content:
In a rapid AI progress scenario—which economists assigned a 14% probability to—economists expect much larger effects:

• Annual GDP growth: 3.3% in 2030, 3.5% in 2050 (roughly comparable to 1992–2001)
• Total factor productivity growth: 2% in 2030, 2.5% in 2050 (close to post-WWII levels)
• Labor force participation rate: 59% in 2030, 55% in 2050 (lower than in the 1950s)

That’s a richer economy, but also one where many fewer people work.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI details rapid AI scenario (14% probability per economists): 3.3-3.5% GDP growth, 2-2.5% TFP growth, but labor force participation dropping to 55% by 2050 - 'a richer economy where many fewer people work.'
items.reasoning: Key finding: even experts see 14% chance of rapid scenario producing post-WWII-level productivity but 1950s-low labor participation. The 'richer but fewer working' framing is crucial for policy.

items.title:
NVIDIA taught AI to skip 99% of a video and understand it better. 

Video models today process every...
items.content:
NVIDIA taught AI to skip 99% of a video and understand it better. 

Video models today process every pixel in every frame. 

That wastes huge compute on pixels that never change. 

A new paper called AutoGaze is a lightweight module that mimics how human eyes actually work. 

It skips redundant backgrounds or repeated content and keeps only what matters. 

It trains by predicting what comes next in a video. Reinforcement learning fine-tunes its choices.
 
> Visual tokens drop by 4 to 100x
> Inference speeds up by up to 19x 
> Processes 1,000-frame 4K footage in real time 

The module plugs directly into existing vision models. 

No full architecture rewrite needed. 

You can now run real-time video understanding on long 4K footage.

items.title: We also asked economists what they thought of six policies that might address the impact of rapid AI...
items.content:
We also asked economists what they thought of six policies that might address the impact of rapid AI progress.

Economists strongly favor targeted measures such as worker retraining, whereas the general public supports both targeted programs and broader interventions, including a job guarantee and universal basic income.

The most favored policy across all respondent groups was retraining support, which economists estimated would lead to a +0.2 p.p. increase in annual GDP growth and a +1 p.p. increase in LFPR in a world with rapid AI progress.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI reports economists favor targeted retraining for AI impact (estimated +0.2pp GDP growth, +1pp LFPR). Public favors broader interventions like UBI and job guarantees.
items.reasoning: Concrete policy findings from expert economist survey. Shows meaningful gap between economist and public preferences on AI policy. Actionable for policymakers.

items.title:
The same AI that wrote your slop code is now reviewing it.

Please, don't call this "code reviews". ...
items.content:
The same AI that wrote your slop code is now reviewing it.

Please, don't call this "code reviews". This is like you grading your own homework.

Every AI tool has blind spots. Every tool makes assumptions, takes shortcuts, and misses edge cases.

What do you think will happen when you ask this same AI tool to review that code?
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino argues AI tools reviewing their own generated code is like 'grading your own homework' - every AI has blind spots and the same tool will miss the same edge cases in review that it missed in generation.
items.reasoning: Strong engagement (7.7k views, 74 likes, 40 replies). Makes an important and original argument about AI code review. The 'grading your own homework' analogy resonates. Directly relevant to the vibe-coding movement's quality concerns.
items.summary_html: Svpino argues AI tools reviewing their own generated code is like 'grading your own homework' - every AI has blind spots and the same tool will miss the same edge cases in review that it missed in generation.
items.content_html:
The same AI that wrote your slop code is now reviewing it.

Please, don't call this "code reviews". This is like you grading your own homework.

Every AI tool has blind spots. Every tool makes assumptions, takes shortcuts, and misses edge cases.

What do you think will happen when you ask this same AI tool to review that code?

items.title: Compounded over decades, small differences in GDP growth can produce large differences in prosperity...
items.content:
Compounded over decades, small differences in GDP growth can produce large differences in prosperity.

Economists’ forecast of 3.5% annual growth rate in the rapid scenario would lead to U.S. economic output of $54.7 trillion in 2050 (real GDP), 25% larger than the $43.7 trillion in the unconditional scenario, where economists predict a 2.5% annual growth rate.

This is roughly equivalent to the difference in U.S. GDP (economic output) between 2016 and today.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI shows compounding effects: economists forecast 3.5% growth in rapid AI scenario leading to $54.7T GDP by 2050 vs $43.7T unconditional - a 25% difference equivalent to 2016-present GDP gap.
items.reasoning: Concrete economic projections putting AI impact in tangible terms. The $11T difference by 2050 is striking.
items.summary_html: FRI shows compounding effects: economists forecast 3.5% growth in rapid AI scenario leading to $54.7T GDP by 2050 vs $43.7T unconditional - a 25% difference equivalent to 2016-present GDP gap.

items.title:
I'm launching a new way to watch the AI community on X tomorrow. 

Damn, it's been a while since I'v...
items.content:
I'm launching a new way to watch the AI community on X tomorrow. 

Damn, it's been a while since I've launched my own thing and my head is playing tricks on me. "What if everyone hates it?" 

Now I understand why so few people ship. 

It's not just a case of building. 

It's a case of controlling your mind. 

But I need it, so even if everyone else hates it, it works for me.

Which also tells me why Woz built a company even after every "expert" in the tech industry told him no one would buy a personal computer. He told me that story personally.

items.title:
RAG is much more than a search engine.

This is one of the most common misconceptions I have to help...
items.content:
RAG is much more than a search engine.

This is one of the most common misconceptions I have to help people get out of their heads.

Think of RAG as a "retrieval + reasoning pipeline" that requires constant evaluation for it to work.

Retrieval without evaluation is just fancy ctrl+F.

Your agents need to judge what they retrieve, not just fetch the top-k nearest vectors.

Here is the TLDR; every RAG system must have a verification layer.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino argues RAG is more than search - it's a 'retrieval + reasoning pipeline' requiring constant evaluation. Says retrieval without evaluation is 'fancy ctrl+F' and every RAG system needs a verification layer.
items.reasoning: Important practical insight about RAG architecture. The distinction between retrieval and retrieval+reasoning is crucial for practitioners. Good engagement (8.5k views). Addresses common misconception.
items.summary_html: Svpino argues RAG is more than search - it's a 'retrieval + reasoning pipeline' requiring constant evaluation. Says retrieval without evaluation is 'fancy ctrl+F' and every RAG system needs a verification layer.

items.title: I met one of the cofounders of @neuralink last week at @hf0 (he's now building a new way to get carg...
items.content:
I met one of the cofounders of @neuralink last week at @hf0 (he's now building a new way to get cargo from China to USA a lot cheaper via autonomous dirigibles like the Goodyear Blimp, but designed and manufactured all with AI's help) and he told me the original team at Neuralink hired very non-traditional people who hadn't worked in neuroscience before.

Steve Jobs when starting the first iPhone team told the first dozen "you are not allowed to hire anyone who has worked on a phone before." (Friend Andy Grignon was one of those first dozen and told me that story).

How do you get different thinking? 

Look outside the box.

items.title: In PDF parsing, it’s super important to output the text in the correct layout (e.g. output tables as...
items.content:
In PDF parsing, it’s super important to output the text in the correct layout (e.g. output tables as tables).

This helps eliminate hallucinations from downstream LLM calls, but ALSO makes it easier to audit with human eyeballs.

With LiteParse we’ve spent a lot of time making sure that the text/table layouts look correct.

Take a look at the screenshots below! A lot of other free/open-source tools (PyPDF, PyMuPDF) output the text in a collapsed list and break table semantics.

Check out the repo: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

If you want higher-accuracy, VLM-enabled parsing, come check out LlamaParse: LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) discusses importance of correct text layout in PDF parsing - tables should stay as tables. Announces LiteParse as open-source solution that preserves layout, unlike PyPDF/PyMuPDF. Also promotes LlamaParse for higher accuracy.
items.reasoning: Addresses a real pain point in RAG pipelines - PDF parsing quality directly affects downstream LLM performance and hallucination rates. Technical substance with practical comparison. From LlamaIndex CEO.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) discusses importance of correct text layout in PDF parsing - tables should stay as tables. Announces LiteParse as open-source solution that preserves layout, unlike PyPDF/PyMuPDF. Also promotes LlamaParse for higher accuracy.
items.content_html:
In PDF parsing, it’s super important to output the text in the correct layout (e.g. output tables as tables).

This helps eliminate hallucinations from downstream LLM calls, but ALSO makes it easier to audit with human eyeballs.

With LiteParse we’ve spent a lot of time making sure that the text/table layouts look correct.

Take a look at the screenshots below! A lot of other free/open-source tools (PyPDF, PyMuPDF) output the text in a collapsed list and break table semantics.

Check out the repo: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

If you want higher-accuracy, VLM-enabled parsing, come check out LlamaParse: LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.title:
Launching an AI agent that reads X on April Fools is stupid. 

But will be interesting to see how mu...
items.content:
Launching an AI agent that reads X on April Fools is stupid. 

But will be interesting to see how much bullshit the AI passes along. 

And then it'll get better on April 2 if it does get fooled. 

Maybe make an April Fools joke tonight and see if you can get your post into my news headlines. 

And Braygent (that's what I call my agent that reads X for me) I will have egg on my face for each one you let through. 

Put them in "Anomolies" section instead. :-)

items.title:
Custom models aren't optional anymore. They are inevitable.

For many use cases, using out-of-the-bo...
items.content:
Custom models aren't optional anymore. They are inevitable.

For many use cases, using out-of-the-box models is not optimal.

But of course, building these custom models is not easy or cheap. Most teams don't even have enough people or time to do this.

Check out what @Koukoumidis from Oumi launched.

They are automating the entire model development lifecycle: evaluation, data synthesis, training, and deployment.

They are calling this #VibeML, which I don't like, but I understand why they are doing it.

You describe what you need, and their platform builds a custom model for you.

This is pretty dope.

items.title:
This is a huge conflict of interest, and you have to be blind not to see it.

Code generation and co...
items.content:
This is a huge conflict of interest, and you have to be blind not to see it.

Code generation and code review are fundamentally different jobs.

Code generation is about producing something that works. Code reviews are about finding the ways that code might break.

Vibe-code is helping us produce crappy code at 100x the speed.

We need a review layer that works more than ever before.

I've been using @QodoAI for at least a couple of years now. They custom-built a multi-agent system to focus on reviewing your code.

But this is what I like about them the most:

1. Qodo processes your entire codebase and pull requests
2. It distills that knowledge into customized rules and patterns
3. It automatically tracks how you follow those over time

This is huge:

It's a system that learns from good code and enforces those rules and patterns everywhere.

This is more powerful than having an AI tool auto-reviewing its own code.

(I can't stop thinking of a company that sells you a drug to make you sick, and then charges you for the medicine you need to get better.)

Here is the link you want to check out:
https://t.co/K636ZVMXHr

Disclosure: I've been working and collaborating with Qodo for a long time.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino argues code generation and code review must be separate - the AI that writes code shouldn't review it. Promotes QodoAI as a dedicated multi-agent code review system that learns patterns from your codebase.
items.reasoning: Important conceptual point about separation of concerns in AI-assisted development. The conflict-of-interest argument (same AI generating and reviewing code) is a genuine concern. Sponsored but substantive.

items.title:
Writing test scripts is dead. AI agents now test your app in a real browser. 

Most testing tools ca...
items.content:
Writing test scripts is dead. AI agents now test your app in a real browser. 

Most testing tools can't see what users see. 

They check functions, not actual browser behavior.

Expect is an open-source CLI repo that fixes this. 

It scans your git diff and hands it to an AI agent.

The agent writes a step-by-step test plan for your changes. 

Then runs every step in a live browser via Playwright.

> Uses your actual login sessions, no auth setup
> Records video of every test run
> Works with Claude Code or Codex 

It fits cleanly into existing CI pipelines. 

Review the plan in your terminal before execution.

No Selenium scripts. No manual clicking through your app. 

One command turns code changes into browser-tested, video-documented QA. 

You just watch the recording when something breaks.

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes Expect, an open-source CLI tool that uses AI agents to automatically write and execute browser tests from git diffs using Playwright, with video recording
items.reasoning: Practical AI-powered developer tool for automated browser testing. Works with Claude Code and Codex. Represents growing trend of AI agents in software QA.

items.title:
Right now, I have a few dozen tools to automate my life.

Every single thing that I do more than onc...
items.content:
Right now, I have a few dozen tools to automate my life.

Every single thing that I do more than once every week is now a tool: either a Claude Code skill, a scheduled workflow, or an application.

• I spend the time doing this once.
• I schedule it.
• I forget about it (or at least, try to)

CREAO is one of the platforms I've used extensively for building some of these automations. They are partnering with me on this post.

They are one of the only platforms where you can go from a conversation to a scheduled agent that quickly.

This is how it works:

1. You describe the problem you want to solve
2. CREAO's agent builds the logic and executes it
3. You can iterate with the agent to improve the solution
4. When you are done, you turn that solution into a mini app
5. You can schedule that app to run any time you need it

Something important:

These agents are deterministic. They don't use an LLM to generate answers, so they will always return the same output given the same input.

This is critical: when you're automating something that runs every Monday at 9 am, you need it to return the same result every time, not a "creative" answer.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino describes his automation philosophy: anything done more than once weekly becomes a tool. Promotes CREAO platform for building deterministic scheduled agents from conversation. Emphasizes deterministic outputs over LLM-generated answers for automation reliability.
items.reasoning: While sponsored, contains genuine insights about the importance of deterministic agent systems vs. creative LLM outputs for production automation. Good engagement at 6.7k views. Reflects broader trend of moving from AI chat to scheduled AI workflows.
items.summary_html: Svpino describes his automation philosophy: anything done more than once weekly becomes a tool. Promotes CREAO platform for building deterministic scheduled agents from conversation. Emphasizes deterministic outputs over LLM-generated answers for automation reliability.
items.content_html:
Right now, I have a few dozen tools to automate my life.

Every single thing that I do more than once every week is now a tool: either a Claude Code skill, a scheduled workflow, or an application.

• I spend the time doing this once.

• I schedule it.

• I forget about it (or at least, try to)

CREAO is one of the platforms I've used extensively for building some of these automations. They are partnering with me on this post.

They are one of the only platforms where you can go from a conversation to a scheduled agent that quickly.

This is how it works:

1. You describe the problem you want to solve

2. CREAO's agent builds the logic and executes it

3. You can iterate with the agent to improve the solution

4. When you are done, you turn that solution into a mini app

5. You can schedule that app to run any time you need it

Something important:

These agents are deterministic. They don't use an LLM to generate answers, so they will always return the same output given the same input.

This is critical: when you're automating something that runs every Monday at 9 am, you need it to return the same result every time, not a "creative" answer.

items.title: The point is, don’t stop measuring after your first benchmark, because accuracy in a vacuum is an in...
items.content: The point is, don’t stop measuring after your first benchmark, because accuracy in a vacuum is an incomplete signal. Benchmark conditions rarely match production conditions, so you have to do the work to keep measuring and learning from actual production data.

items.content:
AI safety has entered the cybersecurity era.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI safety is becoming a cybersecurity issue because advanced AI can now help both defenders and attackers, making the risks more immediate and practical. 

As AI systems become more agentic and more deeply connected to enterprise tools and data, organizations need stronger controls, monitoring, and governance to prevent misuse and reduce exposure.

Read and subscribe for free: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.reasoning: Substantive framing of AI safety as a cybersecurity issue, especially relevant given agentic AI trends. Good engagement (73 likes, 23.6K views). The dual-use nature of AI for both defenders and attackers is an important observation.

items.title: @fogoros @IIoT_World @CRudinschi @Agentic_Factory @antgrasso @Ronald_vanLoon IEC 62443 is not a new ...
items.content:
@fogoros @IIoT_World @CRudinschi @Agentic_Factory @antgrasso @Ronald_vanLoon IEC 62443 is not a new kid on the block. It started as an ISA standard in 2007 and evolved into the broader IEC 62443 series from 2013 onward as a standard. However, for some companies the real shift came for EU in 2025–2026, when some industrial buyers began treating SBOM transparency, vulnerability disclosure, patch responsibility, and IEC 62443 certification as procurement requirements rather than optional security extras required for procurement. However, clients are universally not mandated by law to follow a IEC 62443 certification, but in some regions especially in EU to comply the cyber resilience Act that was adopted in 2004, the scope of full applications actually start in December 2027, and industries the regulations now indirectly push companies toward IEC 62443, and many customers are making it contractually mandatory feature. Therefore, IEC 62443 is not mandatory to comply in United States at least as of now unless it is government related work where there would be an approval that needs to be sought from Federal Government! 

Standards and Technical Reports Included

ISA-TR99.00.01-2007, Security Technologies for Industrial Automation and Control Systems
ISA-62443-1-1-2007, Security for Industrial Automation and Control Systems – Part 1-1: Terminology, Concepts and Models
ANSI/ISA-62443-2-1-2024, Security for Industrial Automation and Control Systems – Part 2-1: Establishing an Industrial Automation and Control Systems Security Program
ISA-TR62443-2-2-2025, Security for industrial automation and control systems, Part 2-2: IACS security protection scheme
ISA-TR62443-2-3-2015, Security for Industrial Automation and Control Systems – Part 2-3: Patch Management in the IACS Environment
ANSI/ISA-62443-3-2-2020, Security for Industrial Automation and Control Systems – Part 3-2: Security Risk Assessment for System Design
ANSI/ISA-62443-3-3-2013, Security for Industrial Automation and Control Systems – Part 3-3: System Security Requirements and Security Levels
ANSI/ISA-62443-4-1-2018, Security for Industrial Automation and Control Systems – Part 4-1: Secure Product Development Lifecycle Requirements
ANSI/ISA-62443-4-2-2018, Security for Industrial Automation and Control Systems – Part 4-2: Technical Security Requirements for IACS Components

https://t.co/AtyfvScSXt
items.author: @gp_pulipaka
items.summary: Detailed explanation of IEC 62443 industrial cybersecurity standard history, its evolution from ISA standard in 2007, and how EU Cyber Resilience Act is indirectly pushing companies toward compliance, particularly in procurement requirements.

Therefore, IEC 62443 is not mandatory to comply in United States at least as of now unless it is government related work where there would be an approval that needs to be sought from Federal Government!

items.title:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of...
items.content:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of my biggest takeaways from my conversation with @karibriski from Nvidia and @Toucas from Mistral AI at GTC.

In the agentic era, the winners will not be the companies running the most pilots.

They will be the ones that can measure what actually works, scale it, and turn it into revenue.

And the ecosystem is evolving fast:

→ Mistral AI announced Forge, enabling enterprises to build and customize their own models using their data and IP

→ Powered by NVIDIA’s accelerated infrastructure

→ NVIDIA also introduced the NEMO Tron coalition to build open models, datasets, and tools

Here’s the breakdown.

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon's lead post from his GTC conversation: enterprises have an evaluation problem, not a model problem. Discusses Mistral AI Forge and NVIDIA NEMO Tron coalition announcements.
items.reasoning: Main thread post with highest engagement (27 likes, 3.6K views). The 'evaluation problem, not model problem' framing is insightful. Reports on Mistral Forge announcement and NEMO Tron coalition from GTC. Clearly sponsored but contains substantive takeaways.

items.title:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, A...
items.content:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, AI telemetry, financial feeds all share the same pattern:

→ continuous ingestion
→ append-only data
→ time-based queries
→ massive retention

Eventually the architecture matters more than tuning.

Tiger Data published the full breakdown here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

Check out the full article : https://t.co/ERSbjhAOKu

items.title:
Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be trav...
items.content:
Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be traversed for each insert
→ Time-series data hits the rightmost index pages
→ Autovacuum activity increases as tables grow

The result is a predictable treadmill.

Add index → queries faster
Write load increases → latency rises
Scale infra → repeat

Tiger Data’s deep dive on this pattern: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.title:
Indexes accelerate reads.

They also multiply writes.

Simple math:

1 insert →

→ 1 heap write
→ +1...
items.content:
Indexes accelerate reads.

They also multiply writes.

Simple math:

1 insert →

→ 1 heap write
→ +1 write per index

So if you have:

→ 5 indexes → 6 writes per insert

At scale:

50k inserts/sec → 300k writes/sec
100k inserts/sec → 600k writes/sec

And that’s before maintenance.

Tiger Data explains the full impact here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.title:
When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + ti...
items.content:
When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + timestamp
→ Partial indexes for recent data
→ Covering indexes for hot queries

Result → queries drop back to milliseconds.

But something important just changed.

Every insert now got more expensive.

Tiger Data breaks this down in detail here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.title:
Most database slowdowns don’t start with an outage.

They start with a “fix.”

You add an index.
Que...
items.content:
Most database slowdowns don’t start with an outage.

They start with a “fix.”

You add an index.
Queries get faster.
Dashboards recover.

Everyone moves on.

But at scale, every new index quietly multiplies your write load.

This is the index trap I discussed with @TigerDatabase.

Tiger Data's deep dive on the optimization treadmill covers the full picture: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

#TigerDataPartner

items.summary_html: @xuwu I've been hearing from developers for years how bad Meta's leadership is. Their display glasses were horrid. Sorry, but I see a bunch of lack of leadership. How they are treating Facebook is abs...
items.content_html:
@xuwu I've been hearing from developers for years how bad Meta's leadership is. Their display glasses were horrid. Sorry, but I see a bunch of lack of leadership. How they are treating Facebook is absolutely horrid. They are letting that rot. I just don't see a lot of care and craftsman ship out of Meta.

And maybe it shouldn't have tried to compete with its own developers and just gone the platform route. If it had, maybe its partners would have been emboldened to pour more resources into it.

That's all on Meta's lack of leadership.

items.title:
How do we build systems where speed and trust can scale together?

I explored this with @MichaelLela...
items.content:
How do we build systems where speed and trust can scale together?

I explored this with @MichaelLeland, field CTO of #island at RSA and it’s the challenge of the AI era.

AI is now an actor. Fast, boundaryless, and creating risks most orgs don’t yet see (hello, shadow AI + agents).

We unpack:
 • AI governance where work happens
 • “No” → “Yes, but” security
 • AI-first architecture

👉 Watch: https://t.co/vHJXrk264H

Check out demos of Island's new AI products here: https://t.co/3mFkBaosjo

#RSAC #Cybersecurity #AI #Governance #IslandPartner #RiskManagement

items.content:
@SadlyItsBradley More:

Sad news over in VR. 

RecRoom was one of the longest-running and most popular on Meta’s VR platform. I haven’t played it for years but spent a lot of time in it.

But the question I have after a raft of closures on Meta’s VR platform:

As it pivots toward AI and glasses will any development house ever trust it again?

The stink coming out of Meta has been really stinky for years now. As a steward of a platform it has been really horrid.

There is something really rotten going on inside Meta. I went back and spent some time on Facebook. It is a real mess.

A real lack of leadership is apparent over there. I know I am actively avoiding using its products and services. When does everyone else?

Might be years but there is a reason I bet on Elon and took shit for years because of it.

So sad just watching what is going on over there though.

items.title:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

A Practical Guide to Reinforceme...
items.content:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).

Amazon Summary:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.

And there aren't any copyright issues here because the training data is all from 1899 or earlier

items.title: @fitzgerald1337 @ray4tesla Andy told me about pitching visual voicemail to AT&T's tech team. It's CT...
items.content:
@fitzgerald1337 @ray4tesla Andy told me about pitching visual voicemail to AT&T's tech team. It's CTO told him "there's no f+++ing way we are building that for you."

Steve Jobs got it done.

I asked AT&T's CEO how Steve got it done.

Made a five-year-exclusive deal for just that one feature. Then the CEO told the CTO to build it. 

Isn't it fun how the real world works?
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer shares detailed insider story about iPhone visual voicemail development - AT&T CTO refused, Jobs negotiated 5-year exclusive deal to force it.
items.reasoning: Interesting tech history anecdote from first-hand sources, decent views but tangential to AI.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer shares detailed insider story about iPhone visual voicemail development - AT&T CTO refused, Jobs negotiated 5-year exclusive deal to force it.
items.content_html:
@fitzgerald1337 @ray4tesla Andy told me about pitching visual voicemail to AT&T's tech team. It's CTO told him "there's no f+++ing way we are building that for you."

Steve Jobs got it done.

I asked AT&T's CEO how Steve got it done.

Made a five-year-exclusive deal for just that one feature. Then the CEO told the CTO to build it.

Isn't it fun how the real world works?

items.title:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI...
items.content:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI internally, so leadership understands it firsthand
→ build with a clear path from pilot to industrialization
→ take control of their own models, data, and evaluation strategy

That is how AI stops being a demo and starts becoming a competitive moat.

And now the tools are here:

Mistral AI Forge makes model customization accessible, powered by NVIDIA infrastructure, while the NEMO Tron coalition is building an open ecosystem to democratize AI development.

Watch more NVIDIA GTC replays at https://t.co/atgeRhMsDH

items.reasoning: Decent enterprise AI strategy content but clearly sponsored (Mistral/NVIDIA partner content). The framework of internal AI use → pilot → industrialization is sound advice.

items.title:
Ironically I'm convinced democracy might kinda work

It just takes 3-5 years of shouting on X about ...
items.content:
Ironically I'm convinced democracy might kinda work

It just takes 3-5 years of shouting on X about an issue, getting told off for it and then suddenly the governments actually do what people said they should do on here

I wonder if you can fasten that feedback loop from 5 years to 5 months or 5 weeks

The reason they are good: they produce deterministic tools, so the same input will always produce the same output.

items.title:
The 4 things I really appreciate about CREAO:

1. It can integrate with any API by pointing to its d...
items.content:
The 4 things I really appreciate about CREAO:

1. It can integrate with any API by pointing to its documentation. This saves a ton of time.

2. Once you build a workflow, it runs the same way every single time. Deterministic outputs are important for many of the things I do.

3. When you describe a new API once, it saves it as a permanent skill that every future app can call without you having to reexplain everything.

4. You can set any mini app to run on a schedule.

Check out CREAO here:
https://t.co/ic4HNNTgWe

items.title: The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a techni...
items.content:
The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a technical preference.

Why?

→ More control over your AI stack
→ Less vendor lock-in
→ Better fit for regulated industries
→ More value from your own enterprise data and IP

General models are strong generalists.

But real differentiation comes when you make AI an expert in your domain, your workflows, your business.

That’s exactly where the market is heading:

With Mistral AI Forge, enterprises can build and fine-tune their own models, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is accelerating open innovation across models, datasets, and tooling.

items.title:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way ...
items.content:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way of building software.

That means leaders need to think in terms of:

→ test cases
→ evaluation frameworks
→ feedback loops
→ continuous optimization

If you cannot define success, you cannot move from POC to production.

That is why so many pilots stall.

At the same time, the foundation is changing:

Mistral AI’s Forge platform now allows enterprises to customize models end-to-end with their own data, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is pushing open AI development with shared models, datasets, and training tools.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon discusses AI as a new paradigm for software building, emphasizing evaluation frameworks, and promoting Mistral Forge and NVIDIA NEMO Tron.
items.reasoning: Third in a sponsored thread. The 'AI is a new way of building software' framing and emphasis on evaluation is valid but promotional.

items.title: I believe it has something to do with the Brazil government and companies following their orders rel...
items.content:
I believe it has something to do with the Brazil government and companies following their orders related to KYC on literally every purchase

Similar to NIF in Portgual

I remember my first week in Portugal, I of course lost my Airpods, so I went to Worten, asked for Airpods, got them, asked to pay, he said what's your NIF, I said what, he said ok your passport number? I said why you need my passport number for buying Airpods? I denied giving it of course! 😂

items.title: 🇧🇷 So @iFood (which is like Brazil's UberEats or DoorDash) invited me to their staff retreat in Bahi...
items.content:
🇧🇷 So @iFood (which is like Brazil's UberEats or DoorDash) invited me to their staff retreat in Bahia

Of course I could not spare you a video of the elaborate Brazilian buffet there 😊😊😊

Bahia is Brazil's most African state, and it's because many of the enslaved Africans arrived here first. The culture is called Afro-Brazilian

So here at this buffet it's rice and beans (as always!) but also 🥘Moqueca

It's a fish stew with shrimp or fish in a base of tomatoes, garlic, coriander, lime and then it is cooked in a clay pot

And Moqueqa is EXTRA interesting because it literally has its origins with the 🪶indigenous of Brazil who called it mukeka, then the 🇵🇹 Portuguese arrived in Brazil and changed the recipe a bit adding onions and coriander, and finally the 🌍 Africans added palm oil and coconut milk to make it their own

So if any dish can show the mix of Brazil's identity it's definitely Moqueca!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio discusses Brazilian food culture, specifically Moqueca, at an iFood staff retreat in Bahia.
items.reasoning: Off-topic (food/culture), not AI-related despite high engagement.

Of course I could not spare you a video of the elaborate Brazilian buffet there 😊😊😊

Bahia is Brazil's most African state, and it's because many of the enslaved Africans arrived here first. The culture is called Afro-Brazilian

So here at this buffet it's rice and beans (as always!) but also 🥘Moqueca

It's a fish stew with shrimp or fish in a base of tomatoes, garlic, coriander, lime and then it is cooked in a clay pot

And Moqueqa is EXTRA interesting because it literally has its origins with the 🪶indigenous of Brazil who called it mukeka, then the 🇵🇹 Portuguese arrived in Brazil and changed the recipe a bit adding onions and coriander, and finally the 🌍 Africans added palm oil and coconut milk to make it their own

So if any dish can show the mix of Brazil's identity it's definitely Moqueca!

items.title: @MarioMigMartins @fba I swear to my mom I write honestly, don't lie about anything and don't post it...
items.content:
@MarioMigMartins @fba I swear to my mom I write honestly, don't lie about anything and don't post it to do ragebait

If you post stuff about other countries like Korea or Singapore you don't get these reactions, they usually react with "yes agreed mostly and that's why we need to improve this"

This is exactly why Portugal is the poorest country in Western Europe now, there's no feedback loop of being able to take criticism, responsibility and improving things

I live here 5 years and it'd flatter you if you'd see my words as what they are: my experience and feedback

I've lived all over the world and have a pretty good track record of being honest about my experiences, and it's my business too with Nomads .com

One thing I notice: the Portuguese outside Portugal generally agree with what I say, the ones inside and who never left Portugal start fighting me
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio giving extensive feedback about Portugal's culture of not accepting criticism, drawing from 5 years living there.
items.reasoning: Off-topic cultural commentary.
items.summary_html: Levelsio giving extensive feedback about Portugal's culture of not accepting criticism, drawing from 5 years living there.
items.content_html:
@MarioMigMartins @fba I swear to my mom I write honestly, don't lie about anything and don't post it to do ragebait

If you post stuff about other countries like Korea or Singapore you don't get these reactions, they usually react with "yes agreed mostly and that's why we need to improve this"

This is exactly why Portugal is the poorest country in Western Europe now, there's no feedback loop of being able to take criticism, responsibility and improving things

I live here 5 years and it'd flatter you if you'd see my words as what they are: my experience and feedback

I've lived all over the world and have a pretty good track record of being honest about my experiences, and it's my business too with Nomads .com

One thing I notice: the Portuguese outside Portugal generally agree with what I say, the ones inside and who never left Portugal start fighting me

items.title: @sampeppard Maritan was a lucky exception in a series of failures. What he did with the Alien preque...
items.content:
@sampeppard Maritan was a lucky exception in a series of failures. What he did with the Alien prequels was beautifully-shot nonsense. Napoleon and Gladiator II are simply unwatchable as movies, but one could make a series of screenshots for the desktop background.

What is considered Quentin's peak is a matter of taste. I think that nothing he has done compares to Pulp Fiction and Kill Bill, not even close.

@sampeppard Maritan was a lucky exception in a series of failures. What he did with the Alien prequels was beautifully-shot nonsense. Napoleon and Gladiator II are simply unwatchable as movies, but one could make a series of screenshots for the desktop background.

What is considered Quentin's peak is a matter of taste. I think that nothing he has done compares to Pulp Fiction and Kill Bill, not even close.

items.title:
James Cameron and Ridley Scott have proven that a genius director must retire at 60.

Age makes a ta...
items.content:
James Cameron and Ridley Scott have proven that a genius director must retire at 60.

Age makes a talented person a master who builds a technically flawless product. But this product isn't built for the public. It's the master doing what they can do best to please themselves.

I hope that Quentin Tarantino stops after his 10th film as he intended to do. Kill Bill was his peak, and it will be sad to start seeing perfectly-made soulless self-copying of an old-man for the sake of it.
